video_title: SpaceX IPO: Inside the Firm That Owns 1%
gist: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW), co-founder of 137 Ventures ($15B AUM), explains the firm's secondary-led strategy of compounding concentrated positions in durable private companies like SpaceX, Palantir, and Anduril, and frames a coming wave of IPOs (SpaceX leading, with OpenAI and Anthropic having confidentially filed S-1s) as the start of late-stage privates returning to public markets.

insights.claim: 137 Ventures was founded in 2011 on the thesis that companies would stay private far longer after the JOBS Act removed the 500-shareholder rule that forced Facebook public, creating a durable, scaling opportunity to invest in great compounders via secondaries.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW), co-founder & managing partner, 137 Ventures
insights.so_what: The structural 'stay-private-longer' tailwind underpins the entire late-stage secondary asset class; investors can treat regulatory change (JOBS Act) as the origin point of today's pre-IPO market.
insights.quote: the major reason why Facebook ended up going public was this 500 shareholder count rule

insights.claim: The secondary market reached over $240 billion in 2025, representing roughly 31% of total venture volume, up by a couple orders of magnitude since 137 Ventures launched in 2011.
insights.speaker: host (Molly O'Shea), confirmed by JFW
insights.so_what: Secondaries are now a third of all venture flow, signaling a structurally large, liquid pre-IPO market that LPs and GPs can no longer treat as a niche.
insights.quote: Over 240 billion was via secondaries. 31% of venture volume was all secondaries.

insights.claim: As companies stay private longer, the liquidity need migrates over time from employees first to investors, expanding the addressable secondary supply across the full cap table.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Buyers of secondaries should expect deal flow to broaden from employee tenders to investor-led blocks as a company ages, changing pricing and sourcing dynamics.
insights.quote: It starts with the employees, but eventually you end up with the investors

insights.claim: 137 Ventures treats primary and secondary investments as interchangeable, caring only about owning durable, compounding companies rather than the deal mechanism.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Signals a manager unconstrained by traditional VC entry rules, able to build positions opportunistically wherever shares become available.
insights.quote: whether or not it's a primary investment or secondary investment, like it honestly doesn't matter that much

insights.claim: SpaceX is positioned to lead a wave of late-stage tech IPOs over the next few quarters, with OpenAI and Anthropic having both confidentially filed S-1s.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Public-market investors should brace for a cluster of mega-cap private companies hitting public markets in a compressed window, a notable supply/sentiment event.
insights.quote: SpaceX is the big one to kind of start this, but I mean, as you mentioned like OpenAI and Anthropic are both filed their S-1s confidentially

insights.claim: 137 Ventures first invested in SpaceX in 2008 while at Founders Fund as the first outside institutional capital, and has since made roughly two dozen separate investments in the company over 16 years.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the firm's follow-on concentration model in practice and explains the reported ~1% SpaceX ownership stake referenced in the title.
insights.quote: we've probably made two dozen investments in the company uh since we started the firm

insights.claim: SpaceX's economic edge came from flipping the launch industry's cost-plus pricing to a firm-fixed-price model and making Falcon 9 partially reusable, fundamentally changing unit economics.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: The reusability + fixed-price combination is the durable moat to underwrite when valuing SpaceX against legacy launch incumbents.
insights.quote: a combination of a business model shift and then making, you know, the Falcon 9 partially reusable fundamentally changed the economics of the business

insights.claim: SpaceX has been profitable on its launch business for a long time and is targeting close to 200 launches this year, versus no competitor exceeding 15-20, meaning it rarely needed to raise capital.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Self-funding launch profitability plus ~10x cadence advantage is the core financial story to model for the IPO, distinguishing SpaceX from cash-burning space peers.
insights.quote: they're going to launch... something close to 200 times. Like, no one else in the world is launching even 15, 20 times

insights.claim: SpaceX has run employee liquidity tenders roughly every six months and buys back its own shares to manage dilution, mirroring public-company capital management while still private.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Regular tenders and buybacks create a recurring, company-organized secondary supply and a transparent reference price ahead of the IPO.
insights.quote: the company has bought back shares, I think, just to manage dilution

insights.claim: SpaceX was valued at roughly $300 billion at a prior reference point having raised only about $10-12 billion in total, underscoring unusually capital-efficient growth.
insights.speaker: host (Molly O'Shea), affirmed by JFW
insights.so_what: A ~$300B valuation on barely ~$10B raised implies extraordinary capital efficiency, a key bull metric for IPO pricing.
insights.quote: the company was valued at 300 billion and hadn't raised as much capital as it has now. I think had raised $10 so far

insights.claim: Starlink became investable around 2019 once early launches proved the constellation could be built economically, with a TAM of tens of millions of people in the US alone who literally cannot buy high-speed internet.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Frames Starlink's addressable market as unserved (not just underserved) demand globally, the growth engine layered on top of profitable launch.
insights.quote: there are tens of millions of people who don't have not that they can't afford it. They literally just can't buy it

insights.claim: Starlink is still underrated in commercial and charter aviation and maritime, with operators willing to ground planes for up to three weeks to install it for connectivity, weather, and crew communications across borders.
insights.speaker: host (Molly O'Shea), supported by JFW
insights.so_what: Transport verticals (aviation, boats) are an under-penetrated, high-willingness-to-pay expansion lane for Starlink revenue beyond consumer broadband.
insights.quote: It's really still very underrated in commercial aviation, and it probably should be on literally every airplane

insights.claim: 137 Ventures invests in companies, not categories, having concluded after 15-20 years of reviewing space deals that SpaceX was the only good space investment and that backing the category winner beats spreading bets across lookalikes.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: A winner-take-most lens argues for concentration in category leaders rather than thematic baskets, directly relevant to how investors should play space, defense, and AI.
insights.quote: I think a lot of people thought, 'Well, we should go invest in all these other things.' And the answer is you should invest in SpaceX.

insights.claim: 137 Ventures avoids sectors with a thousand undifferentiated competitors duking it out for small market shares, seeking instead durable companies likely to capture a large fraction of their market.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: A screen for durability over hype; fragmented, commoditized markets are explicit avoid-signals for this manager.
insights.quote: what you don't want to do is have a thousand different companies that kind of all sound the same and they're all duking it out to end up with like a small piece of the market

insights.claim: 137 Ventures optimizes purely for cash-on-cash returns rather than ownership percentage, citing Peter Thiel's 10% Facebook stake as proof that adjusting exit expectations makes the math work without dogmatic ownership targets.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Reframes the classic VC ownership-target heuristic; managers and LPs can evaluate concentration strategies on absolute return rather than entry ownership.
insights.quote: all I care about is cash on cash returns

insights.claim: 137 Ventures has deliberately not invested in any foundational AI model maker because it cannot predict which model will be best in 10 years, despite acknowledging the technology's massive adoption.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: A notable abstention from the frontier-model race by a sophisticated late-stage investor; the durability of any single LLM leader is treated as unknowable.
insights.quote: we haven't, you know, invested in any of the, you know, big foundational models

insights.claim: Cognition is attractive as the last independent player offering enterprises access to all underlying foundation models without lock-in, analogous to early multi-cloud adoption preferences.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Positions model-agnostic orchestration/independence as the defensible AI investment angle versus betting on a single model provider.
insights.quote: these are the only guys who can give you access to all the underlying models without the lock in

insights.claim: 137 Ventures' edge includes expertise in structuring employee tenders to manage expectations, since running one tender signals more will follow and employees dislike price declines, making cadence and eligibility design critical.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Tender design is a real operational moat in secondaries; mishandled cadence or pricing can damage employee trust and future liquidity programs.
insights.quote: If you run a tender, it turns out your employees are going to assume that you're going to run another tender

insights.claim: Facebook's early right-of-first-refusal model broke when share volumes grew too large to buy back, leading the company to threaten to fire sellers and pushing employees to quit in order to sell, a cautionary tale for late-stage liquidity design.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Illustrates why modern companies build transfer restrictions and structured tenders; poor liquidity policy creates perverse incentives and talent loss.
insights.quote: they basically started telling people like, 'If you sell shares, we're going to fire you.' Right? And so people just started quitting

insights.claim: 137 Ventures backs SpaceX alumni ventures including Impulse Space (Tom Mueller) and Varda (Will Bruey), and holds Hadrian where it has written four to five checks, reflecting its follow-on concentration approach.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Maps the SpaceX talent diaspora as a sourcing network for space/defense bets and shows the firm's multi-check concentration in private names beyond SpaceX.
insights.quote: whether or not it's Hadrian, we've probably written, I don't know, four, maybe five checks there at this point

insights.claim: SpaceX's 'launch and learn' ethos treats test failures as valuable as long as data is gathered, an ethos JFW argues has spread across the hard-tech ecosystem (Starship, Anduril's Fury, Impulse Space).
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Reframes media-reported test failures as expected R&D progress; investors should not penalize iterative hardware testing as execution risk.
insights.quote: I think it's not a failure as long as you're learning something

insights.claim: The US government is an important long-term funding partner alongside venture capital for 137 Ventures' hard-tech portfolio companies, many of which also run large commercial businesses.
insights.speaker: Justin Fishner-Wolfson (JFW)
insights.so_what: Government contracts provide durable demand and non-dilutive capital that de-risk defense/space bets; investors should weigh both commercial and government revenue lines.
insights.quote: venture capital does does put up a lot of dollars, but so does the government
